title: Tokens Per Second
description: The serving metric that measures how quickly a model generates output tokens once streaming has begun.
openingSummary: Tokens per second counts generated output tokens over elapsed generation time. It describes streaming pace for one answer, not queue wait, time to first token, or fleet-wide throughput across many users.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Tokens per second is the rate at which a serving system emits newly generated output tokens during an answer. The count is output tokens divided by the elapsed time spent generating those tokens after streaming begins. A token is a model-specific text unit from the tokenizer, not necessarily one word, so the same sentence can yield different token counts on different models or tokenizers.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Readers feel tokens per second as how fast a streamed reply arrives once it starts. Operators track it separately from time to first token, inter-token latency, and fleet throughput because it answers a different question: how quickly output tokens appear during generation.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Imagine a chat answer that streams one hundred output tokens in five seconds after the first token appears. The tokens-per-second figure for that generation window is twenty output tokens per second. The timer starts when generation begins and stops when the answer finishes or the reader stops the stream.

whatChangesTheNumber.title: What Changes The Number
whatChangesTheNumber.body: Prompt length changes how much prefill work runs before the generation window opens, so a long prompt can delay streaming even though prefill time is usually excluded from tokens per second. Output length changes how many decode steps the answer needs and how long the measured window lasts. Each decode step produces one more token, so a longer answer spends more time in the repeated generation loop. Stopping behavior also matters: an end-of-sequence token, a max-token limit, or a user cancel ends the timer at different points and can make two similar prompts report different rates.

streamingVsThroughput.title: Streaming Pace Versus Fleet Throughput
streamingVsThroughput.body: One reader's streaming pace is the tokens-per-second rate for a single answer during its generation window. Fleet throughput is the total output tokens a deployment serves across many requests and users over the same wall-clock period. A system can show high fleet throughput when it batches many requests together while each individual stream still feels slower. Comparing one user's chat speed to a provider's published throughput figure mixes two different measurements.

servingMetricsCompared.title: Serving Metrics On One Stream
servingMetricsCompared.body: On a single streamed answer, three latency metrics sit beside tokens per second and measure different parts of the experience. Time to first token is the silence before any generated token appears: it covers queue wait, prompt prefill, cache setup, and the first decode step. Inter-token latency is the gap between individual tokens after streaming has started; uneven gaps make the reply feel stuttery even when the average tokens-per-second rate looks fine. Tokens per second averages across the whole generation window after the first token, so it can hide bursty pauses between tokens. Fleet throughput is a separate fleet-level count across many requests and is not shown on this single-stream timeline.

prefillQueueAndDecode.title: Prefill, Queueing, And Decode
prefillQueueAndDecode.body: Tokens per second usually starts measuring after the first output token arrives, but queueing and prefill still shape what the reader experiences before that window opens. Queue wait stretches time to first token when many requests compete for the same accelerator. Prefill processes the full prompt in one larger pass and builds the initial KV cache before steady token generation begins. Decode is the repeated next-token loop that produces each streamed token and fills the tokens-per-second window. A deployment can finish prefill quickly yet still report modest tokens per second if each decode step is slow, or the opposite if decode is fast but queueing and prefill keep answers from starting.

batchingAndScheduling.title: Batching, Scheduling, And Fleet Pace
batchingAndScheduling.body: Batching groups compatible prefill or decode work so one accelerator step serves multiple requests at once. Continuous batching admits new requests and retires finished ones without waiting for a fixed batch to complete. Dynamic batching adjusts batch size as load changes. Request scheduling decides which queued prefill or decode step runs next, and inference-engine policy sets how aggressively those techniques are applied. Together they can raise fleet throughput by keeping hardware busy, but an individual stream may wait longer between turns or share each decode step with other active answers. That tradeoff is why one reader's tokens-per-second pace can fall while the deployment's aggregate output rate rises.

memoryAndKvCache.title: Memory Bandwidth And KV Cache
memoryAndKvCache.body: Each decode step reads the growing KV cache that stores keys and values from earlier positions. As the answer lengthens, more cache state must move through memory for every new token. Memory bandwidth can become the bottleneck that caps tokens per second even when raw compute still looks available. Larger models, longer contexts, and cache-heavy attention patterns increase that pressure. Serving memory limits can also force the runtime to defer, evict, or reload cache blocks, which adds pauses that lower observed streaming pace without changing the model math.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A high tokens-per-second figure does not mean the first token arrived quickly, that every token gap feels even, or that the deployment can sustain the same rate for every user at once. Time to first token, inter-token latency, tokens per second, and fleet throughput each answer a different question on the serving path.

servingPath.title: Serving Path
servingPath.body: Observed tokens per second depends on decode work, cache reuse, batching, and scheduling. Follow prefill for the prompt burst before output begins, decode for the repeated next-token loop, and the prefill/decode split for how serving systems stage the two phases.

links.streamingTimelineTitle: Streaming timeline: where each serving metric applies
links.streamingTimelineLegend: Legend: the top row follows one request from send through queueing and prefill to the first token. The lower row shows decode tokens with gaps between them. Annotations mark time to first token, inter-token latency, and the tokens-per-second measurement window.

streamingTimeline.alt: Timeline of one streamed answer showing queue wait and prefill before the first token, decode tokens with gaps between them, and annotations for time to first token, inter-token latency, and the tokens-per-second window.
streamingTimeline.caption: Serving metrics attach to different parts of one streamed answer: time to first token ends at the first token, inter-token latency measures gaps during decode, and tokens per second averages across the generation window.
